title: Aotearoa Data Explorer – API user guide | Stats NZ
content:
## Aotearoa Data Explorer – API user guide
16 October 2025, 11:00am
This guide explains how to get started with using an Application Programming Interface (API) to request data and metadata from Aotearoa Data Explorer.
For guidance on using the Aotearoa Data Explorer online tool, see:
- Aotearoa Data Explorer user guide
- Quick start guide to Aotearoa Data Explorer .
For tool-specific instructions on retrieving data using the API, see:
- Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in ArcGIS Online
- Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in Excel
- Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in Power BI
- Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in R .
Introduction to the Aotearoa Data Explorer API Subscribe to use the API Overview of API requests Identify an initial URL Data requests Structural metadata requests Request headers Using the API in programming languages More information about API options Service performance Contact us

## Introduction to the Aotearoa Data Explorer API
Aotearoa Data Explorer is a free online tool that presents data in a searchable, flexible, and dynamic way. Data available via the web browser interface can also be requested through the API.
An API is a connection between computer programs that allows them to exchange information. With a few lines of code, programming languages such as R, Python, and JavaScript can request and receive tables or parts of tables from Aotearoa Data Explorer. The API can also be used by applications like Microsoft Excel and Power BI to import data from Aotearoa Data Explorer.
APIs are particularly useful when used repeatedly. For example, where tables are updated monthly or quarterly and the same request can be repeated to source the latest data.
The Aotearoa Data Explorer API uses the SDMX (Statistical Data and Metadata eXchange) standard to define the notation of requests and responses. Requests use simple RESTful URLs and HTTP header options to specify different response formats including XML, JSON, and CSV. These details are explained below.

## Subscribe to use the API
You need to have a subscription to the Aotearoa Data Explorer API service to access data via the API. When making API calls from your applications or systems, you will need to provide a subscription key.
API Portal explains how to sign up and create and manage subscription keys.
How to register through the API Portal and How to use Aotearoa Data Explorer's API are video tutorials on YouTube to help guide you.
When making API calls from your applications and systems, you must provide your subscription key with the HTTP request header Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key .
Request headers has more information about specifying the subscription key.
If an API call is made without a subscription key, status code: 401 will be reported.
Sometimes a previous subscription key will no longer work. Regenerate a new one through the API Portal and use it in your request to resolve the issue.

## Overview of API requests
Data in Aotearoa Data Explorer is available in a series of tables, also known as dataflows. Each table has a unique dataflow ID , which can be specified in API requests.
You can customise your API query by changing parameters to return all or just some of the information from a table, and in the format you want. These details are specified in a URL.
There are two possible base URLs (note that these will not work without more detail, which is explained later in this document):
- https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/
- http://apis.stats.govt.nz/ade-api/rest/
This document usually references URLs based on the first, as that is what is provided in the Aotearoa Data Explorer Developer API query builder in the browser. Documentation in Stats NZ’s API Portal may reference the second. Both are interchangeable for the example requests shown in this document.
Aotearoa Data Explorer API requests fall into two types:
- structural metadata requests.
Structural metadata includes information such as table titles, table descriptions, and codelists with item labels.
These requests are explained below.

## Identify an initial URL
To see what tables are available and to determine an initial URL for an API call, you can use either the Aotearoa Data Explorer browser or the API catalogue. We recommend using the browser for those less experienced with APIs, or for users wanting just some of the data in a table.

### Aotearoa Data Explorer browser
Aotearoa Data Explorer browser lets you search or browse topics to find a table of interest. Opening a table in the browser lets you customise what is shown on screen by selecting and deselecting items from filters.
Quick start guide to Aotearoa Data Explorer shows how to use the browser to find and customise tables.
Open the Developer API option above the table to get the URL templates for:
- Data query , which will return the data as shown in your Aotearoa Data Explorer table, including any filters applied
- Structure query , which will return the full structural metadata for the table.
You may want to customise these URLs using options later in this document.
A summarised catalogue of tables in Aotearoa Data Explorer is available using the following request:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/dataflow/STATSNZ/all?detail=allstubs
A more detailed catalogue including table dimensions is available using:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/dataflow/STATSNZ/all
These return table titles and the corresponding details including:
- Dataflow ID , for example, "AGR_AGR_001"
- AgencyID , for example, "STATSNZ"
- Version , for example, "1.0"
These values can be used in the API requests explained below.
API requests for data are in the format:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/{agencyID}{dataflowID}{version}/{key}?{queryStringParameters}
The organisation publishing the data, for example “STATSNZ”.
The organisation publishing the data, for example “STATSNZ”.
This is mandatory and identifies the table being requested using its unique ID, for example “AGR_AGR_001”.
This is mandatory and identifies the table being requested using its unique ID, for example “AGR_AGR_001”.
The version of the table, for example “1.0” or “latest”. If not specified this defaults to the latest.
The version of the table, for example “1.0” or “latest”. If not specified this defaults to the latest.
Specifies what data to return from the table. Use “all” for all data from the table.
Specifies what data to return from the table. Use “all” for all data from the table.
QueryStringParameters
QueryStringParameters
Specify options such as a response format. If not specified, the response format defaults to XML.
Specify options such as a response format. If not specified, the response format defaults to XML.
These parameters are explained in more detail below with common options and examples.

### Request all data from a table
All data from a table can be requested using the following format:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/{agencyID},{dataflowID}/all
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/{agencyID},{dataflowID}/all
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STATSNZ,AGR_AGR_001/all
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STATSNZ,AGR_AGR_001/all
In the case above, the version defaults to the latest. Alternatively, this can be specified:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/{agencyID,{dataflowID},{version}/all
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/{agencyID,{dataflowID},{version}/all
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STATSNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STATSNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all
These requests will return data in XML format by default.
Specify the response format explains how to output data in other formats, for example, CSV.
Some tables contain millions of data points (for example, the biggest 2023 Census tables have around 45 million data points) and take a while to extract. Requesting only selected data from a table can reduce the file size and transfer time.

### Request selected data from a table
The {key} parameter is optional and lets you request only some data points from a table.
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/{agency},{dataflowID},{version}/{key}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/{agency},{dataflowID},{version}/{key}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STATSNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/.1+2.2018
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STATSNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/.1+2.2018
An easy way to determine the URL for a partial table is to design a customised view in Aotearoa Data Explorer browser and copy the Data query URL from the Developer API menu above the table. This URL will include any filters applied.
Alternatively, URLs can be written using parameter values identified from the browser, structural metadata, or previous API calls.
- use . between codes from different dimensions
- use + between codes from the same dimension
- specifying no values for a dimension will return data for all of its codes.
The example from the screenshot below, taken from the Aotearoa Data Explorer browser, requests selected data from the table AGR_AGR_001: Forestry by Regional Council . This table has three dimensions: Forestry, Area, and Year.
The {key} value within the data query is “.1+2.2018”. The “.” symbols separate the three dimensions. The dimension values are:
Notation within {key}
Notation within {key}
Blank (nothing before the first “.”)
Blank (nothing before the first “.”)
1 (Northland) and 2 (Auckland)
1 (Northland) and 2 (Auckland)

### Specify the response format
By default, information will be returned in XML format. You can optionally include {queryStringParameters} in the URL to request a specific format:
{queryStringParameters}
{queryStringParameters}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=xml
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=xml
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=jsondata
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=jsondata
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=csv
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=csv
CSV (file with codes and labels in same column)
CSV (file with codes and labels in same column)
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=csvfile
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=csvfile
CSV (file with codes and labels in different columns)
CSV (file with codes and labels in different columns)
format= csvfilewithlabels
format= csvfilewithlabels
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=csvfilewithlabels
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/data/STAT SNZ,AGR_AGR_001,1.0/all?format=csvfilewithlabels

## Structural metadata requests
API requests for structural metadata are in the format:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/{structure}/{agencyID}{resourceID}{version}?{queryStringParameters}
The type of structure the request is for, for example, a “dataflow” (table) or a “codelist”.
The type of structure the request is for, for example, a “dataflow” (table) or a “codelist”.
The organisation publishing the structure, for example “STATSNZ”.
The organisation publishing the structure, for example “STATSNZ”.
The ID of the structure being requested, for example, the codelist ID. If not specified this defaults to all instances of the structure, for example, all codelists held in Aotearoa Data Explorer.
The ID of the structure being requested, for example, the codelist ID. If not specified this defaults to all instances of the structure, for example, all codelists held in Aotearoa Data Explorer.
The version of the structure, for example “1.0” or “latest”. If not specified this defaults to the latest.
The version of the structure, for example “1.0” or “latest”. If not specified this defaults to the latest.
QueryStringParameters
QueryStringParameters
Specify options such as the level of detail.
Specify options such as the level of detail.
These parameters are explained in more detail below with common options and examples.

### Request all structural metadata for a table
All structural metadata related to a table can be requested using:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/dataflow/{agencyID}/{dataflowID}?references=all&detail=referencepartial
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/dataflow/{agencyID}/{dataflowID}?references=all&detail=referencepartial
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/dataflow/STATSNZ/AGR_AGR_001?references=all&detail=referencepartial
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/dataflow/STATSNZ/AGR_AGR_001?references=all&detail=referencepartial

### Request selected structural metadata for a table
Different types of structural metadata can be requested separately using different {structure} values. The most useful {structure} values include:
Details about a table including its title and description, the dimensions it contains, the dimension order, and the related codelistIDs.
Details about a table including its title and description, the dimensions it contains, the dimension order, and the related codelistIDs.
Details about a table including its title and description, and its dimensions. Less detailed than datastructure, but the default option in the ADE browser.
Details about a table including its title and description, and its dimensions. Less detailed than datastructure, but the default option in the ADE browser.
The codes and labels for items within a dimension, for example a list of country codes and names.
The codes and labels for items within a dimension, for example a list of country codes and names.
The topic(s) a table belongs to, for example “Agriculture”.
The topic(s) a table belongs to, for example “Agriculture”.
If requesting information for a datastructure, dataflow, or categorisation then the {resourceID} parameter should be the table’s dataflow ID:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/{structure}/{agencyID}/{dataflowID}/{version}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/{structure}/{agencyID}/{dataflowID}/{version}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/datastructure/STATSNZ/AGR_AGR_001/1.0
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/datastructure/STATSNZ/AGR_AGR_001/1.0
If requesting information for a codelist then the {resourceID} parameter should be the codelist ID:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/codelist/{agencyID}/{codelistID}/{version}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/codelist/{agencyID}/{codelistID}/{version}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/codelist/STATSNZ/CL_AREA_AGR_AGR_001/1.0
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/codelist/STATSNZ/CL_AREA_AGR_AGR_001/1.0
Structural metadata can be requested for all tables or codelists at once using “all” for the {resourceID}, or by not specifying a {resourceID} which defaults to all.
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/{structure}/{agencyID}/all/{version}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/{structure}/{agencyID}/all/{version}
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/datastructure/STATSNZ/all/latest
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/datastructure/STATSNZ/all/latest

### Specify the query parameters
Multiple types of {queryStringParameters} are available for structural metadata. More than one can be used in a request if separated by “&”.
A “references” option allows information for related structures to be returned with the primary structure requested in the {structure} parameter. For example, if requesting a “datastructure” for a table you can also ask for the “codelist” structures used in the table. Useful options are:
{queryStringParameters}
{queryStringParameters}
No information for related structures.
No information for related structures.
Returns information for all structure types related to the primary structure.
Returns information for all structure
types related to the primary structure.
references={structure}
references={structure}
For example, references=codelist. Returns information for the structure specified here as well as the primary structure.
For example, references=codelist.
Returns information for the structure
specified here as well as the primary structure.
A “detail” option can be used to specify how much detail is returned. Useful options are:
{queryStringParameters}
{queryStringParameters}
Detail=referencepartial
Detail=referencepartial
Only returns information for related structures used in the table specified.
Only returns information for related structures used in the table specified.
Returns information for other structures even if not used in the table specified.
Returns information for other structures even if not used in the table specified.
The following example uses both a reference and detail parameter to return dataflow and codelist information for table AGR_AGR_001, with the codelist information only including codes used in the table:
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/dataflow/STATSNZ/AGR_AGR_001/1.0?references=codelist&detail=referencepartial
https://api.data.stats.govt.nz/rest/dataflow/STATSNZ/AGR_AGR_001/1.0?references=codelist&detail=referencepartial
More information about API options below has links to documentation about more structural metadata options.
When making an API request to Aotearoa Data Explorer, your request will need to include a request header which at minimum, specifies your subscription key (see Subscribe to use the API ). Other request header commands are optional.
Useful options include:
Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key
Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key
Mandatory, to specify your subscription key generated in the Stats NZ API Portal.
Mandatory, to specify your subscription
key generated in the Stats NZ API Portal.
Enables compression. Accept-encoding values include “identity” (no compression), “compress”, and “gzip”.
Enables compression. Accept-encoding
values include “identity” (no compression), “compress”, and “gzip”.
Specifies the level of metadata to be returned. Options are “all”, “upperOnly”, “currentOnly”.
Specifies the level of metadata to be returned. Options are “all”, “upperOnly”, “currentOnly”.
A value of “no-cache” means that cached versions of the requested resource cannot be used without first checking to see if there is an updated version.
A value of “no-cache” means that cached versions of the requested resource cannot be used without first checking to see if there is an updated version.
Identifies the client making the request. The standard format of this header is: <application>/<application version> (Language=<language>/<language version>). For example, MyApp/1.0 (Language=Python/3.13).
Identifies the client making the request.
The standard format of this header is: <application>/<application version>
(Language=<language>/<language version>). For example, MyApp/1.0 (Language=Python/3.13).
The following screenshot shows an HTTP example with a request URL and some of the header options above.
How to use Aotearoa Data Explorer's API shows an example request with the Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key header using the Postman API Platform .
To learn more about the many header options available, we recommend searching “HTTP headers” on the web.

## Using the API in programming languages
The method or notation to request information from an API differs depending on the software or programming language being used.
Aotearoa Data Explorer API in our data portal includes a code generator for common programming languages such as HTML, Python, and JavaScript. To use the code generator, choose a request type from the left-hand list (1 in the image below), then click Try it on the far right (2 in the image below).
The Try it button will open a panel where you can enter details to include in your request. Near the bottom of the panel is an “HTTP request” section with a drop-down to select a language. Example code will be shown under this.
Note this code is automatically generated and some options may not work for an Aotearoa Data Explorer table.

## More information about API options
This guide covers only some of the available options.
The Aotearoa Data Explorer REST API is fully compliant with SDMX 2.1 – the Statistical Data and Metadata eXchange information model. Useful SDMX references with more detail include:
- .Stat SDMX RESTful Web Service Cheat Sheet in documentation for the .Stat Suite (the platform used by Aotearoa Data Explorer)
- SDMX-REST Guide on GitHub
- SDMX cheat sheet on GitHub
- SDMX RESTful API 1.5 standard on GitHub.
Note that some options may not work with the Aotearoa Data Explorer API or selected tables.
Stats NZ strives to maintain availability of the API, but we cannot guarantee uninterrupted service. The performance and availability of the API may be affected during periods of high usage, maintenance activities, or unforeseen technical issues. In the event of any issues, Stats NZ will work to resolve them as soon as possible.
Stats NZ may email API subscribers to notify them of current problems or scheduled outages.
If you've got a question or feedback about our API service, contact our Information Centre.
Phone: 0508 525 525 (toll free within New Zealand) +64 4 931 4600 (from outside New Zealand)
Email: info@stats.govt.nz
Use our online contact form .
- Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in ArcGIS Online 16 October 2025
- Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in Excel 16 October 2025
- Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in Power BI 16 October 2025
- Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in R 16 October 2025
